Draft · July 2026 · By James Mann
Forecasting the Remote Labor Index

The RLI is a proxy for how much real remote work AI can do. Forecasting it well could provide insight into the impact of AI on the economy in the near future, however, forecasting is hard. We attempt this using various existing methods and propose our own.
The question

For all their many flaws, benchmarks are one of the best methods we have for tracking the trajectory of AI capabilities. In addition to allowing us to track the pace of progress up to now, another important use of benchmarks is in calibrating us about what to expect in the future. For instance, the METR time horizons work established a long running historical trend in the length of software engineering tasks that models could complete 50% of the time and by projecting that trend out predicted the seismic advances that followed.
What if we could do the same for the RLI?
A story of increasing complexity
Our first approach is naive: fit a sigmoid to the points, project it out. The simplest possible solution forms our baseline for all subsequent methods.
Pimpale et al

Our next approach aligns more closely to work from some MATS scholars, whereby you first fit a line from time to a general capability metric – they use Arena elo, we opt for the ECI – then go from that capability metric to the benchmark scores. This fit moves away from time, which is contingent on release cycles, into a more generalised capability domain which we hope is smoother relative to the RLI.

Baked into this fit is an assumption about the rate of progress in capabilities continuing as it has since ~April ’25; there is no guarantee this is the case, things could hit a wall and slow down, or alternatively, things could speed up. Experiment with the slider below to see how the pace of progress affects the forecast.
Does this even work?

These approaches seem extremely weak, how well should we expect them to perform? One way to answer this question is to explore how well would they have performed in benchmarks that have since saturated. To do this we gather 30 benchmarks from Epoch AI and Artificial Analysis and find the frontier curves for them all. We show each benchmark individually below:

Aggregating and normalising these various trends into a single graph gives us something like a saturation rate distribution. This can be considered either by being indexed by date or by capability (ECI). We can then fit sigmoids to each of these lines to smooth them out a little and make them nicer to play with.

This gives us the range of ways that previous benchmarks have come to saturate, we can then begin to test whether our previous methods would have worked well on this set. To do so we can wind the clock back to put them all into a similar regime, taking the earliest handful of relatively low scoring models and seeing if applying our methods approximates well the eventual outcome. Our comparison metric is the distributions of MAE on the final fits, with the MAE on fits made with access to only datapoints within X% of the minimum. Explore below, the way the two distributions eventually converge as more data is allowed to be used in the fits.
This becomes easier to visualise when you consider the lines it is actually producing.

It seems that this approach, despite its simplicity, retains some amount of power. Even in very low data regimes, the lines we get out are often reasonably indicative of the overall trajectory of the benchmark.

We can use these past trajectories to contextualise our predictions on the RLI. They show that our naive sigmoids, should they come to pass, would place the RLI’s saturation on the more aggressive side, though not inconceivably so.
Difficulty Distributions

This brings us to the limit of what naive score extrapolation can provide. To this point, we have spoken little about the underlying causes of the variation in saturation rates, treating them as inscrutable facts of the benchmarks themselves. This need not be the case, however, the source of this variation can be pegged very exactly, it is a result of the underlying variation in the set of tasks which make up the benchmark.

Consider a toy benchmark, ToyBench, it consists of 100 tasks, each of which can be modelled as a binary outcome over whether a given model under test M possesses capability CM > D. The distribution of tasks’ D is directly linked to the saturation rate of ToyBench. Consider a uniform but wide distribution, in that case saturation will be slow as capabilities grind their way through the domain. Consider, instead, a distribution with majority of its mass around a peak, saturation will occur very suddenly, as capabilities moves through the peak and vast swathes of the benchmark get solved all at once. This idea can be explored below, change the shape of the distribution and watch how the saturation curve varies with it.

One interesting thing to note from this is that despite our empirical findings and general consensus, benchmarks are not intrinsically solved in a sigmoidal fashion. This can instead be attributed to the fact that existing benchmark construction ends up with gaussian shaped task difficulty distributions, which necessarily biases curves towards being sigmoid-ish. Should distributions instead be uniform, we would find trajectories which are far more linear.

It should be clear how this distribution could help score forecasting, in the first instance, it’s intrinsic to the benchmark and so we wouldn’t have to wait for models to score highly to understand the shape of this benchmark (in theory, in practice it’s slightly harder). Additionally, it enables us to predict sudden rushes or slows before they happen, or diagnose them after they happened. This is because a change can be one of two things (1) genuine step changes and discontinuities in model capabilities (2) unnatural groupings of task difficulties which all get surpassed at once. Disambiguating this is at present hard, difficulty-oriented benchmarking offers a solution.

To understand how, we must leave our toy setting and attempt to derive difficulty statistics for real benchmarks. This is a challenge. Creating such statistics requires task-level information, which is far less common than aggregate statistics. Fortunately, this exists for a couple benchmarks and so we can work with them. The pipeline is as follows: (1) Fit the models ECI on all aggregate results to determine their respective capability scores (2) Rate each task against those fits to get a task specific curve.
This gives us an insight into the overall distributions of difficulty, in standard ECI units, of the tasks within several famous benchmarks.

With these distributions found, we can begin to forecast performance. At this point, we make the simplifying assumption that any model with capability C will solve tasks with demand less than C. From this we arrive at a splendidly stupid way to model frontier performance growth, simply by treating these distributions as PDFs and integrating them into CDFs. The graph below demonstrates the power of this technique, it’s important to remember that the shape of these lines emerged without fitting to the actual trajectory.

Note that this approach performs better for certain benchmarks than for others, SimpleQA Verified and Chess Puzzles both are modelled noticeably more poorly than the other benchmarks, why could this be? We believe that this is the result of the ECI not capturing the thing that these benchmarks test, we show the correlation between model’s benchmark specific scores and their overall ECI against the error in the fit below:

Why is this? Well one hypothesis is that the ECI catches something like a general capability, where SimpleQA and Chess Puzzles are slightly more niche, in this way the ECI underestimates how quickly they progress because it is blind to progress in certain capabilities which matter specifically to those benchmarks. One way to improve on this method would be to account for the multi-dimensional nature of capability, we leave this to later work.

To recap up to now, we have shown that most benchmarks saturation can be well modelled after the fact as a sigmoid, however predicting this sigmoid in advance is difficult. We find that modelling progress along a capability axis instead of time makes it appear smoother. We suggest that in the low data regime, when the upwards trajectory is yet to reveal itself, finding the difficulty of the tasks within the benchmark provides some signal about the overall trajectory (when the benchmark is closely correlated with the chosen capability axis). We now return to our original question, forecasting the RLI.
Task-difficulty based modelling of the RLI
The RLI does not give us access to item level scores, however they do include in the paper a graph from which we can derive distributions over human completion time and cost.

At this stage we have to make another jump, we need to get from cost or completion time to ECI units, one possible way to do this is via METR’s time horizons work, as we have derived ECIs for their various tasks, and METR provide completion time labels. Joining these suggests a strong relationship between the two, we use the derived formula to convert our completion time distribution into ECI units.

This distribution, as the keener reader may have noticed, is incorrect given what we know about the ECI and the current frontier on the RLI. There are a couple possible explanations for this, one is that METR care much more about eliciting the top end of a model’s capabilities so as to best upper bound its performance; the RLI takes the opposite approach, setting much lower caps on time and token budget. Another possible factor is that METR tasks, which are all software engineering, being more familiar to models and play more to their strengths. Weighting these factors without seeing the actual tasks and attempts is impossible, but either way we must correct for this within our distribution. We find that applying a uniform shift to align model scores with cumulative readings accounts for the entire discrepancy, however note that this is not an ideal method.
With these steps all completed, we can sketch out a task-based difficulty forecast for the RLI. Again, explore how accelerating and

Three methods, each different, one totally dissimilar to the other two, produce a consistent forecast for the RLI showing it move rapidly upwards during 2027 before saturating in 2028. We do not speculate about the consequences of our forecast, should it come to pass; others have dealt with this topic in greater depth and we recommend their analysis.
Limitations

The limitations of the initial two methodologies, direct time fitting and the two step ECI fitting, are that they are disconnected from the underlying benchmark. That said, historical evidence indicates that they have performed well in the past. The third methodology, our task-difficulty approach, aims to resolve this by considering the shape of the underlying benchmark.

This is weak in two ways, for the benchmarks we have task level data for, there is a circularity in our evaluation, in that we rely on future models to determine the difficulty of individual tasks. There is, as yet, no method for determining the difficulty of a task that no model has completed. We attempt to mitigate this by excluding the benchmark we are projecting from the ECI parameter fitting, however, this does not escape the fact that the task difficulties themselves are contaminated with models from the future. We think there is an opportunity for exciting work in circumventing this by approximating difficulties of the standalone task, for example as was done in General Scales, or through some methodology that looks both at the standalone task and failing model attempts.

The second issue, specific to the RLI where we avoid this somewhat by using time and cost labels which are intrinsic to the samples themselves, is that it is not clear the extent to which time and cost are good proxies for difficulty, METR’s dataset, and our intuition suggests they should be related; but without access to the underlying benchmark this is hard to say for certain. There is also the less principled than could be desired requirement for shifting implied ECIs. This problem is, again, hard to solve without better access to the underlying benchmark.
Conclusion

We present an attempt at forecasting scores on the RLI, we find that various different methods, which have been validated to work when backtested on previous benchmarks, converge on rapid improvement through 2027.
